Do not add numbers that are not present in the table.
Do not claim causality.
Use plain language suitable for a non-technical audience.
This protects against a common risk: fluent overinterpretation.
The output should be checked against the original table before being shared.

title: Data Analysis Support Agent
text:
Data Analysis Review Checklist
Before using an AI-supported analysis output, the human analyst should review:
Question
[ ] Is the analytical question clearly stated?
[ ] Is the decision or purpose of the analysis clear?

Data
[ ] Is the data source documented?
[ ] Is the unit of analysis clear?
[ ] Are key variables defined?
[ ] Are missing values checked?
[ ] Are duplicates checked where relevant?

Methods
[ ] Is the chosen method appropriate for the question?
[ ] Are assumptions stated?
[ ] Are limitations documented?

Code
[ ] Does the code run in the intended environment?
[ ] Are file paths and outputs correct?
[ ] Are transformations inspectable?
[ ] Are results reproducible?

Outputs
[ ] Are tables and plots labeled clearly?
[ ] Do results match the analysis question?
[ ] Are interpretations supported by evidence?
[ ] Are uncertainty and limitations included?

Communication
[ ] Is the language clear?
[ ] Are claims cautious?
[ ] Is human review complete before sharing?
This checklist turns AI support into accountable analysis practice.

title: Data Analysis Support Agent
text:
Common Failure Modes
AI-supported data analysis can fail in predictable ways.

Failure mode 1: The agent assumes column meanings
A column named score may refer to many things.
It could be an assessment score, risk score, model score, satisfaction score, or quality score.
The agent should not assume meaning without metadata.


Failure mode 2: The agent writes code that looks right but does not run
Generated code can contain wrong package names, wrong function arguments, or objects that do not exist.
The human must test the code.


Failure mode 3: The agent overstates the result
A descriptive comparison may be written as if it proves impact.
The human must check whether the study design supports the claim.


Failure mode 4: The agent ignores missingness
Missing data can change interpretation.
A responsible analysis should report missingness and explain how it was handled.


Failure mode 5: The agent produces polished but unsupported language
Professional writing is not the same as valid interpretation.
The final narrative must be checked against the evidence.

title: Data Analysis Support Agent
text:
Documentation Standard
Every AI-supported analysis should leave a small record.
A simple documentation note can include:
AI use record

Project:
Date:
Human analyst:
AI role:
Input materials used:
Outputs generated:
Checks performed:
Known limitations:
Final human decision:
This does not need to be complicated.
The purpose is to make the workflow inspectable.
If someone later asks how the result was produced, the team should be able to explain the process.

title: Data Analysis Support Agent
section: How This Connects to CDI Data Science Guides
text:
How This Connects to CDI Data Science Guides
In the CDI ecosystem, data analysis is not treated as isolated computation.
It is part of a system:
Question
  ↓
Data
  ↓
Analysis
  ↓
Interpretation
  ↓
Communication
  ↓
Decision
The data analysis support agent fits into this system as an assistant.
It can help learners and professionals use the Data Science Foundations, Applied Data Science, Omics Systems, Career System, and other CDI guides more effectively.
However, the same principle remains:

AI may support the workflow, but the evidence must carry the claim.

This is especially important in scientific, medical, educational, financial, and policy settings.

title: Data Analysis Support Agent
text:
Practical Exercise
Choose one small dataset or results table.
Use the data analysis support agent pattern to prepare a review plan.
Your output should include:
1. Analytical question
2. Dataset description
3. Unit of analysis
4. Key variables
5. Proposed checks
6. Suggested analysis steps
7. Expected outputs
8. Interpretation cautions
9. Human review checklist
Do not begin with code.
Begin with the system.
After the plan is clear, then code becomes easier to write, test, and explain.

title: Data Analysis Support Agent
text:
Chapter Summary
A data analysis support agent helps a human analyst work more clearly and carefully.
It can support question framing, data understanding, analysis planning, code development, quality checks, interpretation, communication, and documentation.
But it must remain bounded.
It should not invent data, fabricate results, make unsupported claims, or replace human responsibility.
The strongest use of this agent is not to speed through analysis blindly.
It is to make analysis more inspectable, reproducible, and explainable.

title: Data Analysis Support Agent
text:
Looking Ahead
The next chapter introduces the Communication and Reporting Agent.
That agent focuses on turning reviewed work into clear messages, reports, briefs, slides, documentation, and public-facing communication.
The same principle continues:
AI can help shape communication, but human review must protect accuracy, context, tone, and responsibility.

title: Communication and Reporting Agent
text:
Purpose of a Communication and Reporting Agent
Communication is one of the most common ways people use AI.
They ask AI to draft emails.
They ask AI to summarize documents.
They ask AI to rewrite reports.
They ask AI to turn technical notes into plain language.
They ask AI to prepare presentations, announcements, captions, and explanations.
This is useful.
But communication is not only writing.
Communication carries meaning, evidence, audience expectations, professional judgment, and responsibility.
A communication and reporting agent should therefore not be treated as a tool for producing polished words only.
It should be treated as a system for helping humans communicate clearly, honestly, and appropriately.
A communication and reporting agent helps transform information into useful messages for specific audiences.
Its purpose is to support:
The agent can assist with many communication tasks, including:
However, the agent should not decide what should be communicated without human oversight.
It should not invent evidence.
It should not exaggerate results.
It should not remove uncertainty.
It should not make claims that the human cannot defend.
The agent supports communication.
The human owns the message.

title: Communication and Reporting Agent
text:
clearer writing,
better structure,
appropriate tone,
audience adaptation,
transparent reporting,
evidence-based summaries,
and reusable communication workflows.



drafting emails,
rewriting messages,
summarizing findings,
preparing reports,
converting technical language into plain language,
generating slide outlines,
preparing public updates,
writing learner feedback,
and creating project documentation.

title: Communication and Reporting Agent
section: Where the Agent Fits in the Human–AI–Human Workflow
text:
Where the Agent Fits in the Human–AI–Human Workflow
The communication and reporting agent follows the same Human–AI–Human model used throughout this guide.
Human defines the communication purpose
        ↓
Human provides audience, context, evidence, and constraints
        ↓
AI drafts, rewrites, summarizes, structures, or adapts the message
        ↓
Human reviews accuracy, tone, completeness, and risk
        ↓
Human approves, edits, sends, publishes, or archives the final output
The workflow begins with human intent and ends with human approval.
This is important because communication can affect trust.
A technically correct message can still be inappropriate for the audience.
A polished report can still overstate the evidence.
A friendly email can still create the wrong expectation.
A summary can omit important limitations.
For that reason, the agent must be designed with review built in from the beginning.

title: Communication and Reporting Agent
text:
Common Use Cases
A communication and reporting agent can support many recurring professional tasks.

Email and Message Drafting
The agent can help draft:

professional emails,
follow-up messages,
reminders,
announcements,
thank-you notes,
mentorship messages,
project updates,
and stakeholder responses.

For these tasks, the agent needs the relationship, purpose, tone, and required action.
A good input might include:
Audience: A student mentee
Purpose: Congratulate them for creating a GitHub account
Tone: Warm, encouraging, simple, mentor-like
Must include: Keep learning, keep building, portfolio mindset
Avoid: Too much technical language
Signature: Teresia Mrema Buza, Mentor, JIONGEZE Youth Skills Mentorship, Founder, Complex Data Insights
This gives the agent enough structure to produce a useful draft.
The human still reviews whether the message sounds genuine and appropriate.


Research and Analysis Summaries
The agent can help turn long notes into:

executive summaries,
literature summaries,
finding summaries,
methods summaries,
interpretation notes,
and limitation statements.

For these tasks, the agent must be grounded in source material.
It should not summarize from memory when evidence is required.
A useful instruction is:
Use only the notes provided below.
Do not add claims that are not supported by the notes.
Separate findings, limitations, and next steps.
Mark any unclear point as needing human review.
This keeps the output closer to the available evidence.


Reports and Documentation
The agent can help prepare structured reports from project outputs.
For example:
Inputs:
- analysis summary table
- methods notes
- interpretation notes
- figures
- audience description

Outputs:
- report title
- short background
- methods summary
- key findings
- limitations
- recommendations
- appendix checklist
This is especially useful in data science, omics, clinical data, career, and AI systems projects.
The agent helps organize the report.
The human verifies the claims.


Public Communication
The agent can help convert internal work into public-facing communication such as:

LinkedIn posts,
website descriptions,
repository descriptions,
README introductions,
project announcements,
learning reflections,
and program updates.

Public communication requires extra care.
The agent should avoid overclaiming.
It should respect privacy.
It should avoid presenting early work as finished evidence.
It should make the purpose of the work clear.

title: Communication and Reporting Agent
text:
Required Inputs
A communication and reporting agent works best when the human provides clear input.
The minimum input package should include:
Communication purpose:
Audience:
Relationship to audience:
Main message:
Evidence or source material:
Required details:
Tone:
Length:
Format:
Things to avoid:
Review criteria:
Without these inputs, the agent may produce generic writing.
Generic writing can sound polished but fail the real task.
For example, a message to a student should not sound like a corporate press release.
A scientific report should not sound like a marketing caption.
A professional update should not overstate uncertain results.
A communication agent must adapt to the situation.

title: Communication and Reporting Agent
text:
Communication Context Package
For repeatable communication tasks, it helps to create a context package.
A context package contains the stable information that the agent needs repeatedly.
For example:
Brand or organization:
Complex Data Insights

Communication style:
Clear, practical, professional, human-led, educational

Common audiences:
Learners, researchers, analysts, educators, mentors, professionals

Preferred principles:
- Evidence before claims
- Human responsibility before automation
- Clarity before complexity
- Reproducibility before speed
- Respectful and encouraging tone

Signature style:
Teresia Mrema Buza | Complex Data Insights
This package can be reused across reports, emails, guides, and announcements.
It helps the agent maintain consistency.
However, the context package should not replace task-specific instructions.
Each communication task still needs its own audience, purpose, and evidence.

title: Communication and Reporting Agent
text:
Output Standards
A communication and reporting agent should produce outputs that are:

accurate,
clear,
audience-aware,
appropriately toned,
evidence-based,
complete enough for the purpose,
transparent about uncertainty,
and ready for human review.

The output should also match the requested format.
For example, if the user asks for an email, the output should look like an email.
If the user asks for a report section, the output should read like a report section.
If the user asks for a summary, the output should not become a long essay.
If the user asks for a public caption, the output should be concise and readable.
Format is part of quality.

title: Communication and Reporting Agent
text:
Review Checklist
Before sending, publishing, or submitting AI-assisted communication, the human should review the output.
A practical checklist is:
Accuracy:
Does every factual claim match the available evidence?

Audience:
Is the language appropriate for the reader?

Tone:
Does it sound respectful, professional, and human?

Completeness:
Does it include the required information?

Privacy:
Does it avoid exposing sensitive or unnecessary details?

Uncertainty:
Does it avoid overstating confidence?

Action:
Is it clear what the reader should know, do, or decide?

Ownership:
Would I be comfortable signing, sending, publishing, or defending this?
The final question is the most important.
If the human would not be comfortable owning the message, the message is not ready.

title: Communication and Reporting Agent
text:
Reporting Agent Workflow
A reusable reporting agent can follow this workflow.
1. Receive the reporting purpose
2. Identify the audience
3. Review the supplied evidence
4. Separate facts, interpretations, limitations, and recommendations
5. Draft the report structure
6. Generate the first draft
7. Flag unsupported claims or missing information
8. Apply tone and format requirements
9. Produce a review checklist
10. Wait for human approval before final use
This workflow makes reporting more reliable.
It also prevents the agent from jumping too quickly into polished writing before the evidence is understood.

title: Communication and Reporting Agent
text:
Example Agent Specification
A reusable communication and reporting agent can be described like this:
Agent name:
Communication and Reporting Agent

Purpose:
Help transform human-provided notes, evidence, and instructions into clear communication outputs.

Primary users:
Learners, researchers, analysts, mentors, educators, and professionals.

Supported outputs:
Emails, summaries, reports, announcements, README sections, social posts, slide outlines, and documentation drafts.

Required inputs:
Audience, purpose, source material, tone, length, format, required details, and review criteria.

Boundaries:
Do not invent facts.
Do not exaggerate findings.
Do not remove important uncertainty.
Do not publish or send without human approval.
Do not expose private or sensitive information unnecessarily.

Review requirements:
Human must check accuracy, tone, completeness, privacy, and actionability before use.
This specification makes the agent easier to reuse and improve.

title: Communication and Reporting Agent
text:
Example Prompt Template
A practical prompt template is:
You are helping me prepare a communication output.

Purpose:
[Explain why this message or report is needed]

Audience:
[Describe who will read it]

Context:
[Provide background information]

Source material:
[Paste notes, findings, tables, messages, or draft text]

Required content:
[List details that must be included]

Tone:
[Professional, warm, concise, encouraging, formal, plain-language, etc.]

Format:
[Email, report section, summary, README, announcement, slide outline, etc.]

Constraints:
[Length, words to avoid, privacy concerns, claims not to make]

Review task:
Before giving the final draft, flag any missing information, unsupported claims, or risk areas.
Then provide a clean draft for human review.
This template supports both writing and review.
It reminds the agent that communication quality depends on evidence, audience, and responsibility.

title: Communication and Reporting Agent
text:
Risks and Failure Modes
Communication agents can fail in several ways.
They may produce writing that is too generic.
They may make the message sound less human.
They may overstate results.
They may remove important limitations.
They may invent details.
They may use a tone that does not fit the relationship.
They may expose information that should remain private.
They may make a draft sound final before it has been reviewed.
These failures are not only writing problems.
They are system problems.
The solution is to design the workflow with grounding, constraints, and review.

title: Communication and Reporting Agent
text:
Human Judgment in Communication
Human judgment is especially important in communication because people respond not only to information, but also to tone, timing, relationship, and trust.
An AI system may help write a message.
It cannot fully understand the relationship behind the message.
It may help summarize a report.
It cannot take responsibility for how the report is used.
It may help polish a public statement.
It cannot know every social, professional, or ethical implication.
For that reason, communication should always return to human responsibility.
The human decides what is true.
The human decides what is appropriate.
The human decides what should be sent.
The human decides what should be published.

title: Communication and Reporting Agent
section: Connection to the Full AI Systems Guide
text:
Connection to the Full AI Systems Guide
This chapter completes the applied agent section of the guide.
The full guide has moved through four layers:
AI Foundations
        ↓
AI Systems
        ↓
Reusable AI Agent Template
        ↓
Applied AI Agents
The communication and reporting agent brings these ideas together.
It uses foundations because communication must remain human-led.
It uses systems thinking because communication work has inputs, workflows, outputs, review points, and risks.
It uses reusable agent design because communication tasks often repeat.
It becomes applied because real people need real messages, reports, summaries, and documentation.

title: Communication and Reporting Agent
text:
Chapter Summary
A communication and reporting agent helps humans turn information into clear, audience-aware outputs.
It can support emails, reports, summaries, announcements, public posts, documentation, and presentations.
Its value is not only speed.
Its value is structure, clarity, consistency, and review support.
However, the agent must not invent evidence, exaggerate findings, or replace human approval.
Responsible communication remains grounded in human judgment.
The final message should always be something the human is willing to own.

title: Communication and Reporting Agent
text:
Looking Ahead
The main chapters of the AI Systems guide are now complete.
The next section moves into appendices and references.
The appendix can provide reusable templates, checklists, agent specification formats, evaluation rubrics, and workflow examples.
The references section can document the sources and broader ideas that support responsible, human-led AI systems.

title: Appendix
text:
Appendix A: Human–AI–Human Workflow Checklist
This appendix provides reusable reference materials for the AI Systems guide.
The main chapters explain the ideas.
This appendix turns those ideas into practical tools.
Use these templates to design AI workflows, review AI outputs, document AI use, and build reusable AI agents that remain human-led.
Use this checklist before relying on an AI-supported output.

title: Appendix
text:
Human--AI--Human Workflow Checklist

1. Human framing
   [ ] The problem or question is clearly stated.
   [ ] The intended audience is known.
   [ ] The purpose of the output is clear.
   [ ] The risk level of the task is understood.

2. Context and grounding
   [ ] Relevant background information has been provided.
   [ ] Key data, documents, examples, or references have been included where needed.
   [ ] Important constraints are stated.
   [ ] Unavailable information is acknowledged.

3. AI assistance
   [ ] The AI task is specific.
   [ ] The expected output format is clear.
   [ ] The AI is not being asked to make final human decisions.
   [ ] Sensitive, private, or unsafe information is handled carefully.

4. Human evaluation
   [ ] The output has been checked for correctness.
   [ ] The output has been checked for completeness.
   [ ] The output has been checked for bias, risk, or misleading claims.
   [ ] Claims have been verified where needed.

5. Human decision and documentation
   [ ] The final decision remains human-led.
   [ ] AI use has been documented where appropriate.
   [ ] Limitations are communicated honestly.
   [ ] The output is saved, revised, or rejected based on review.

title: Appendix
section: Appendix B: AI Task Framing Template
text:
Appendix B: AI Task Framing Template
Use this template before starting an AI-supported task.
AI Task Framing Template

Task title:

Purpose:

Who will use the output?

What decision, action, or learning goal should this support?

Background context:

Input materials provided:

Constraints:

Expected output format:

Quality criteria:

Known risks:

What the AI should not do:

Human review steps:

Final owner of the decision:
A task that is poorly framed usually produces weak output.
A task that is clearly framed gives the AI system a better chance of producing something useful and reviewable.

title: Appendix
text:
Appendix C: Prompt Design Template
A good prompt is not only a question.
It is a small workflow specification.
Prompt Design Template

Role:
Act as ...

Goal:
Help me ...

Context:
Here is the situation ...

Inputs:
Use the following information ...

Constraints:
Do not ...
Prioritize ...
Assume ...

Process:
First ...
Then ...
Finally ...

Output format:
Return the answer as ...

Review requirement:
Before finalizing, check for ...
This structure helps move prompting away from guessing and toward system design.

title: Appendix
text:
Appendix D: Context Package Template
Context packages are useful when AI needs to work with documents, data, examples, or project-specific information.
Context Package Template

Project name:

Task:

Relevant background:

Audience:

Source material included:

Important definitions:

Important examples:

Data notes:

Style or formatting requirements:

Things to avoid:

Uncertainties:

Expected output:
A context package should help the AI stay grounded.
It should also help the human reviewer understand what the output was based on.

title: Appendix
section: Appendix E: AI Output Evaluation Rubric
text:
Appendix E: AI Output Evaluation Rubric
Use this rubric to review AI outputs before using them.









Criterion
Low quality
Acceptable
Strong




Correctness
Contains clear errors or unsupported claims
Mostly correct but needs review
Accurate, checked, and well-supported


Relevance
Does not answer the task
Partly answers the task
Directly addresses the task


Completeness
Missing important pieces
Covers the basics
Covers key details without unnecessary excess


Clarity
Confusing or poorly structured
Understandable
Clear, organized, and easy to use


Evidence
No grounding or unclear sources
Some grounding
Claims are traceable to appropriate evidence


Risk awareness
Ignores possible harm or limitations
Mentions some limitations
Clearly identifies limits, uncertainty, and risk


Human usability
Hard to act on
Usable with edits
Ready for human review and adaptation



The goal is not to make every AI output perfect.
The goal is to decide whether the output is safe, useful, and appropriate for the task.

title: Appendix
section: Appendix F: AI Use Documentation Template
text:
Appendix F: AI Use Documentation Template
Use this when documenting AI support in learning, research, analysis, writing, or professional work.
AI Use Documentation Template

Project or task:

AI tool used:

Date of use:

Purpose of AI support:

Input materials provided to AI:

Type of output generated:

Human review performed:

Corrections or changes made by human reviewer:

Limitations identified:

Final decision owner:

Notes for reproducibility:
Documentation does not have to be complicated.
It should make the workflow understandable and defensible.

title: Appendix
section: Appendix G: Reusable AI Agent Specification Template
text:
Appendix G: Reusable AI Agent Specification Template
Use this template to define a reusable AI agent before building or using it.
Reusable AI Agent Specification

Agent name:

Agent purpose:

Primary users:

Supported tasks:

Tasks outside scope:

Required inputs:

Optional inputs:

Context sources:

Memory rules:

Workflow steps:

Decision logic:

Escalation conditions:

Output formats:

Review requirements:

Quality standards:

Safety boundaries:

Version:

Last reviewed:
An agent without scope can become unreliable.
An agent with clear scope can become a repeatable workflow component.

title: Appendix
section: Appendix H: Agent Output Review Checklist
text:
Appendix H: Agent Output Review Checklist
Before accepting an agent output, review it against this checklist.
Agent Output Review Checklist

[ ] The output matches the agent purpose.
[ ] The output stays within the agent scope.
[ ] The output uses the provided context appropriately.
[ ] The output does not invent unsupported information.
[ ] The output follows the required format.
[ ] The output is understandable to the intended audience.
[ ] The output identifies uncertainty where needed.
[ ] The output avoids unsafe or inappropriate recommendations.
[ ] The output is ready for human editing, approval, or rejection.
[ ] The final human decision is documented.
Agent review is not a sign of failure.
It is part of responsible agent design.

title: Appendix
section: Appendix I: AI Workflow Log Template
text:
Appendix I: AI Workflow Log Template
A workflow log helps teams and learners track how AI was used.
AI Workflow Log

Date:

Task:

Human owner:

AI role:

Inputs used:

Prompt or workflow summary:

Output received:

Review findings:

Changes made:

Final outcome:

Lessons learned:
Over time, workflow logs help improve prompts, templates, review standards, and agent behavior.

title: Appendix
text:
Appendix J: Risk Level Guide
Not all AI tasks carry the same level of risk.
Use this simple guide to decide how much review is needed.








Risk level
Examples
Review requirement




Low
Brainstorming titles, outlining notes, summarizing personal study ideas
Light human review


Medium
Drafting reports, interpreting non-sensitive data, preparing public communication
Careful review and editing


High
Medical, legal, financial, employment, academic grading, safety-critical, or sensitive personal decisions
Expert review, source verification, and strong human oversight



High-risk tasks should never be treated as fully automated AI tasks.
They require human expertise, documentation, and careful judgment.

title: Appendix
section: Appendix K: Example Human-Led AI Workflow
text:
Appendix K: Example Human-Led AI Workflow
The following example shows how a responsible AI workflow can be documented.
Task:
Prepare a short explanation of a data analysis result for a non-technical audience.

Human framing:
The analyst identifies the result, audience, purpose, and limitations.

Context provided:
Clean summary table, chart, project goal, and notes about what the analysis can and cannot claim.

AI assistance:
The AI drafts a plain-language explanation.

Human review:
The analyst checks whether the explanation matches the data, removes overclaims, and adds limitations.

Final output:
A revised explanation that can be shared in a report.

Documentation:
The analyst records that AI was used for drafting language, not for generating the statistical result or final interpretation.
This pattern can be adapted to research, learning, communication, coding, mentoring, and portfolio development.

title: Appendix
section: Appendix L: CDI AI Systems Principle Summary
text:
Appendix L: CDI AI Systems Principle Summary
The AI Systems guide is built around a few core principles.
1. AI should assist human thinking, not replace human responsibility.
2. AI work should begin with a clear human-framed problem.
3. Context improves output quality.
4. Grounding improves reliability.
5. Evaluation is part of the system, not an afterthought.
6. High-risk work requires stronger review.
7. Reusable agents need clear purpose, scope, inputs, outputs, and maintenance.
8. AI-supported work should be documented when it influences decisions or public outputs.
9. Human judgment remains central.
10. Responsible AI practice is learned through repeatable workflows.
These principles connect all four parts of the guide:
AI Foundations
        ↓
AI Systems
        ↓
Reusable AI Agent Template
        ↓
Applied AI Agents

title: Appendix
text:
Closing Note
Appendices are not just extra material.
In a systems guide, they are part of the operating infrastructure.
They help learners and teams move from ideas to repeatable practice.
Use these templates, adapt them, improve them, and keep them connected to human judgment.
The next section provides references and source material for further reading.

title: References
text:
How References Are Used in This Guide
This page collects the references used throughout the AI Systems guide.
The guide uses a bibliography file stored at:
Quarto automatically uses this file because it is declared in _quarto.yml:
References support the guide in four main ways.
First, they connect AI systems practice to established work on artificial intelligence, human judgment, automation, evaluation, and responsible technology use.
Second, they help learners see that AI workflows should not be treated as isolated tool tricks. They belong to a broader ecosystem of research, documentation, governance, learning, communication, and professional responsibility.
Third, they provide a foundation for future expansion. As AI tools, standards, and regulations change, the bibliography can be updated without changing the structure of the guide.
Fourth, they model good practice. A responsible AI workflow should document where ideas, claims, definitions, methods, and frameworks come from.

title: References
text:
Suggested Reference Categories
The bibliography for this guide may include sources related to:

artificial intelligence foundations,
human-centered AI,
responsible AI,
AI evaluation,
human-in-the-loop systems,
automation and decision support,
AI literacy and education,
prompt design and context engineering,
data governance,
algorithmic accountability,
AI safety,
reproducible workflows,
documentation practices,
and professional AI use.

title: References
text:
Adding a New Reference
To add a new source, place a BibTeX entry inside:
library/references.bib
A simple example is:
@book{example2026,
  title     = {Example Title},
  author    = {Author, Firstname},
  year      = {2026},
  publisher = {Example Publisher}
}
Then cite it inside any chapter using:
[@example2026]
Quarto will collect cited sources and display them on the references page.
